name: Hajj & Umrah
epithet: The adhkar of the pilgrim through the rites
title: Hajj & Umrah Du'as: the adhkar of the pilgrim through the rites in Arabic, transliteration and meaning
meta_description: The pilgrim's adhkar through the rites of Hajj and Umrah: talbiyah, tawaf, sa'i, Arafah, Muzdalifah and the jamarat, in Arabic, transliteration and English with sources.
keywords: Hajj dua, Umrah dua, talbiyah, dua for tawaf, dua for sa'i, dua on Arafah, Muzdalifah dua, jamarat, pilgrim adhkar, Hisn al-Muslim, Buruja
lede: Hajj is a journey the body makes, but it is the tongue that keeps the heart in step. From the moment you enter ihram, the rites move you from one station to the next, and at each one the Prophet ﷺ left words: a call answered, a verse recited, the oneness of Allah declared on a mountain. To know the adhkar is to walk the path the way it was walked.

This is the wird of the pilgrim, following the rites in order: the talbiyah of ihram, the remembrance of tawaf, the climb of Safa and Marwah, the great standing at Arafah, the night at Muzdalifah, and the stoning at the jamarat. Whether you are on your way or longing from afar, let these words walk you through it.
intro: These follow the order of the rites. The talbiyah (number 233) begins at ihram and is repeated again and again until you stone the first pillar. At the Black Stone during tawaf you say Allahu Akbar (234). Between the two corners you recite the verse 'Rabbana atina' (235). On Safa and Marwah you make the remembrance of the sa'i (236). On Arafah you say the best of all du'a (237), at Muzdalifah you stand in remembrance facing the qiblah (238), and at the jamarat you say Allahu Akbar with each pebble (239).

Recite the Arabic, lean on the transliteration while you learn it, and read the meaning so your heart is in each station with your feet. Between every prescribed word, the door of personal du'a is wide open: ask Allah for whatever you long for.
closing: The whole of Hajj is one long answer to a call. 'Labbayk Allahumma labbayk', here I am, again and again, from the first step in ihram to the last pebble thrown. These adhkar are how the pilgrim says, at every station, that he came because Allah called and he wants nothing but Him. Learn them, and the journey becomes what it was always meant to be: a body in motion and a heart that never stops returning.
O Allah, write our names among the pilgrims to Your House, accept from us as You accepted from those before us, and let us answer Your call with a heart that means every word of it.

faq.q: What is the talbiyah and when do I say it?
faq.a: The talbiyah is 'Labbayk Allahumma labbayk' (du'a 233), the pilgrim's answer to Allah's call: 'Here I am at Your service.' You begin it the moment you enter ihram and repeat it abundantly, raising your voice with it, until you stone the first pillar (Jamratul-Aqabah) on the day of Eid.

faq.q: What do you say during tawaf around the Ka'bah?
faq.a: There is no fixed du'a for the whole of tawaf; you may ask Allah for anything. At the Black Stone you say Allahu Akbar (du'a 234), and between the Yemeni corner and the Black Stone you recite the verse 'Our Lord, grant us good in this world and good in the next, and save us from the punishment of the Fire' (du'a 235).

faq.q: What is the best du'a to say on the Day of Arafah?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ said the best du'a is the du'a of the Day of Arafah, and the best thing he and the prophets before him said was the declaration of Allah's oneness in du'a 237. Arafah is the heart of Hajj; spend it asking, facing the qiblah, with raised hands.

email.subject: The adhkar of the pilgrim, station by station
email.alt_subject: Walking the rites the way they were walked
email.preview: Talbiyah, tawaf, sa'i, Arafah, Muzdalifah and the jamarat, with Arabic, transliteration and meaning.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Hajj is a journey the body makes, but it is the tongue that keeps the heart in step. From the moment you enter ihram, the rites move you from one station to the next, and at each one the Prophet ﷺ left words: a call answered, a verse recited, the oneness of Allah declared on a mountain.

We have gathered the wird of the pilgrim, following the rites in order, each with its Arabic, a transliteration to help you learn it, the meaning, and its source. The talbiyah of ihram, the remembrance of tawaf, the climb of Safa and Marwah, the great standing at Arafah, the night at Muzdalifah, and the stoning at the jamarat.

The whole of Hajj is one long answer to a call: 'Here I am, O Allah, here I am.' These adhkar are how the pilgrim says, at every station, that he came because Allah called. Whether you are on your way or longing from afar, let them walk you through it.

The full collection, formatted to read or recite, is here:

social.hook:
Hajj is a journey the body makes.

But it's the tongue that keeps the heart in step.
social.caption:
The adhkar of the pilgrim, station by station.

From the moment you enter ihram, the rites move you from one place to the next, and at each one the Prophet ﷺ left words: the talbiyah of ihram, the remembrance of tawaf, the climb of Safa and Marwah, the great standing at Arafah, the night at Muzdalifah, and the stoning at the jamarat.

The whole of Hajj is one long answer to a call. Labbayk Allahumma labbayk: here I am, again and again, from the first step to the last pebble.

Save this, and read or recite the full collection with Arabic, transliteration and meaning at buruja.com/adhkar/hajj-umrah

233.title: The talbiyah
233.note: The pilgrim's answer to Allah's call, 'Here I am at Your service.' Begun at ihram and repeated abundantly, with the voice raised, until the first pillar is stoned. The sound of Hajj itself.

234.title: At the Black Stone
234.note: During tawaf, each time he passed the corner of the Black Stone the Prophet ﷺ would point toward it and say Allahu Akbar. The remembrance that marks every circuit around the House.

235.title: Between the two corners
235.note: Recited during tawaf between the Yemeni corner and the Black Stone: the Qur'anic verse asking for good in this world and the next and refuge from the Fire (al-Baqarah 2:201), the most comprehensive request a heart can make.

236.title: On Safa and Marwah
236.note: The remembrance of the sa'i. Facing the Ka'bah on each hill, the Prophet ﷺ declared Allah's oneness and greatness, affirmed that He kept His promise and aided His servant, then made personal du'a, repeating it three times on each.

237.title: The du'a of Arafah
237.note: The Prophet ﷺ said the best du'a is the du'a of the Day of Arafah, and the best of what he and the prophets before him said was this declaration of Allah's oneness. The heart of the entire Hajj.

238.title: At Muzdalifah
238.note: At al-Mash'ar al-Haram the Prophet ﷺ faced the qiblah and stood in du'a, magnifying Allah and declaring His oneness, until the sky grew light, leaving just before sunrise.

239.title: At the jamarat
239.note: The Prophet ﷺ said Allahu Akbar with each pebble thrown at the three pillars. After the first and second he would step forward, face the qiblah, raise his hands and make du'a; after the last he left without standing.
